Hood Design and Application: Hood type inappropriate for process

The current hood type does not match the process requirements. Review the process and contaminant cloud characteristics, and replace with an appropriate hood type (enclosing, receiving, or capturing) as per the classification system to ensure effective control, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
Insufficient degree of enclosure

The hood does not adequately enclose the source, reducing control effectiveness. Increase the degree of enclosure by adding walls, barriers, or converting to a partial or full enclosure to improve containment and reduce required airflow, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
Hood too small for contaminant cloud size

The hood opening is insufficient to receive or capture the full extent of the contaminant cloud. Enlarge the hood face area to match the size and shape of the contaminant cloud, or reposition the hood closer to the source, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
Hood positioned too far from source

The hood is located beyond the effective capture distance (typically >2 hood diameters for capturing hoods). Reposition the hood to within one to two hood diameters of the source to ensure effective capture of the contaminant cloud, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
Working zone extends beyond capture zone

The area where work is performed extends outside the hood's capture zone, allowing contaminant to escape. Mark the capture zone clearly on the workstation and train operators to work within it, or install an adjustable hood or workstation to maintain the working zone within the capture zone, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
Hood lacks flanges

The capturing hood has no flange, reducing capture efficiency and effective reach. Fit flanges to the hood to restrict airflow from behind, create a larger capture zone, improve air velocity distribution, and increase the hood entry coefficient, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
Hood face velocity too low

The measured face velocity is insufficient to contain or capture the contaminant cloud for the application. Increase the extraction volume flow rate to achieve the minimum required face velocity for the hood type and process conditions (minimum 0.4 m/s for partial enclosures, or as per Table 9 for capturing hoods), as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
Flow separation and excessive eddying at hood entrance

Airflow separation and recirculatory eddies at the hood entrance reduce hood efficiency and can cause contaminant leakage. Install a flared or tapered inlet to smooth airflow, reduce flow separation, and minimise recirculatory eddies. Consider adding flanges to improve entry characteristics, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
Hood orientation does not account for contaminant cloud direction

The hood is not positioned to take advantage of the natural speed and direction of the contaminant cloud. Reorient or relocate the hood to intercept the contaminant cloud along its natural trajectory, particularly for receiving hoods and directional sources, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
Missing or inadequate barriers between source and breathing zone

There is insufficient physical separation between the contaminant source and the operator's breathing zone. Install transparent barriers, screens, or modify the booth design to create physical separation whilst maintaining visibility and access for the work process, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
Wake effect causing exposure

The operator's position at the hood face creates a wake region where contaminant accumulates before being drawn into the breathing zone. Redesign the workstation to move the source away from the hood face and operator, or convert to a side-draught or downdraught configuration to minimise the wake effect, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
Hood not matched to how work is actually performed

The hood design does not accommodate the actual working methods and operator movements. Observe work practices, consult with operators and supervisors, and redesign the hood application using ergonomic principles to match how the work is actually carried out. Consider prototype testing, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
Insufficient clearance time for booth/enclosure

The time required for contaminated air to clear from the enclosure after the source stops has not been established or is excessive. Measure and establish the clearance time, display it prominently, ensure extraction continues until clearance time has elapsed, and inform all personnel. Consider design modifications to reduce eddies and clearance time, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
Missing or inadequate turntables/jigs for directional processes

Processes producing directional, high-speed contaminant clouds lack means to limit operator ability to direct the cloud outside the enclosure. Install turntables, jigs, or positioning devices to control workpiece orientation and prevent contaminant clouds being directed out of the partial enclosure. Mark correct working positions, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
Airflow and Extraction: The measured extraction volume flow rate is below the design specification or insufficient for effective control. Investigate causes (blockages, fan issues, leaks) and restore the required volume flow rate. Verify system balance and adjust dampers if necessary. Consider fan upgrade if system capacity is insufficient, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

The air velocity in ductwork is insufficient to keep particles in suspension, causing settlement and potential blockages. Increase the air velocity to meet the minimum required transport velocity for the contaminant type (refer to Table 12). This may require reducing duct diameter or increasing fan capacity, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

Workplace draughts are disrupting the hood's capture zone or causing contaminant leakage from enclosures. Identify and suppress draught sources (open doors/windows, cooling fans, vehicle movements). Consider increasing hood face velocity to resist draughts, or add partial enclosure walls to shield the source, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.

The enclosure is not maintained at sufficient negative pressure relative to the workplace, risking outward leakage of contaminated air. Increase extraction flow rate, seal gaps in the enclosure fabric, and verify negative pressure is maintained. Provide and maintain overpressure alarms, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.

Airflow distribution across multiple branches does not match the design specification, causing some hoods to under-perform whilst others over-extract. Commission a competent person to rebalance the system by adjusting dampers to achieve the specified flow rate at each hood. Document the correct damper settings, as per HSG258 Chapter 7 and Chapter 8.
Dampers incorrectly adjusted or tampered with

Dampers have been moved from their correct settings, disrupting system balance and hood performance. Reset dampers to the correct positions as per the commissioning report. Consider restricting operator access to dampers, or provide clear instructions and supervision on their correct use, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
System airflow significantly below design specification

Overall system performance has degraded substantially from the commissioning benchmark. Conduct a systematic investigation to identify causes (filter blockage, fan deterioration, duct leaks, partial blockages). Rectify identified faults and restore system to design performance. Consider thorough examination and test by a competent person, as per HSG258 Chapter 10.
Ductwork: Excessive bends or sharp bends

The ductwork contains too many bends or sharp-angled bends causing particle accumulation, increased resistance, and potential blockages. Replace sharp bends with gradual radius bends (R not less than 1.5 times duct diameter). Minimise the total number of bends. Provide access panels at bends for inspection and cleaning, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
T-junctions present

The ductwork uses T-junctions which cause turbulence, particle drop-out, and uneven flow distribution. Replace T-junctions with Y-junctions having smooth, gradual joining angles to improve airflow characteristics and reduce particle settlement, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Horizontal duct runs too long

Extended horizontal ductwork sections increase the risk of particle settlement and blockage. Minimise horizontal run lengths where possible. Ensure transport velocities are adequate for the materials being conveyed. Increase frequency of inspection and cleaning for remaining horizontal sections, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Inadequate duct wall thickness for material being conveyed

The ductwork material thickness is insufficient for the abrasive or corrosive nature of the contaminant, leading to wear or damage. Replace affected ductwork sections with material of appropriate thickness and specification for the duty (refer to Table 11 for guidance on galvanised steel thickness), as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Flexible ducting excessive or damaged

Long lengths of flexible ducting present high flow resistance, low resilience, and are easily damaged. Replace flexible sections with rigid ducting wherever practicable. Limit flexible duct use to short connection lengths only. Replace any split or damaged flexible sections immediately, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Missing or inadequate drainage points

The system lacks drainage points for condensates, aerosols, or liquids, risking accumulation and progressive duct blockage. Install drainage points with appropriate valves at all low points in the ductwork. Establish and implement a regular drainage schedule as part of the maintenance programme, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Duct blockages or particle accumulation

Dust, debris, or product buildup is reducing airflow through the duct system. Inspect the ductwork and remove any blockages to restore correct airflow throughout the LEV system. Review transport velocities and consider design modifications if blockages recur. Increase inspection and cleaning frequency, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Leaks in ductwork

Air leakage through duct joints, seams, or damage is reducing system performance and potentially releasing contaminant to the workplace (especially on positive pressure sections). Seal all leaks in ductwork, particularly in positively pressurised sections. Replace damaged sections. Ensure all joints are properly flanged and sealed, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Ductwork violates building fire compartments

The ductwork penetrates fire compartment boundaries without appropriate protection, compromising building fire safety. Ensure ductwork complies with fire compartment integrity requirements. Install appropriate fire dampers, barriers, or protection as required by building regulations, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Missing or inaccessible inspection/access panels

The ductwork lacks adequate access points for inspection, testing, and cleaning, hindering maintenance and fault diagnosis. Install leak-proof inspection covers at key points in the duct system, particularly at bends, after junctions, and at low points. Ensure access points are accessible and simple to open, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Thermal expansion/contraction not accommodated

The ductwork design does not allow for thermal movement, risking joint failure or structural damage. Install slip joints or other expansion accommodation measures appropriate for the expected temperature range. Review joint types and materials specification, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Fan Air Mover: The fan is operating outside its efficient duty point range, leading to increased noise, power consumption, and risk of system failure. Adjust system resistance using dampers or variable speed controls to move the operating point to within the fan's optimal range. Consider fan replacement if operating conditions have changed significantly from original design, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

The fan type does not suit the application requirements (pressure, flow, contaminant type). Replace with an appropriate fan type: radial blade for heavy dust loads, backward bladed for high pressures, or as required for the specific application and contaminant characteristics, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

The fan is positioned such that indoor ductwork is under positive pressure, risking contaminant leakage into the workplace through any duct leaks. Relocate the fan to place as much ductwork as possible under negative pressure, particularly within occupied buildings. Consider locating fans and positively pressurised sections outside occupied areas, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Inadequate fan capacity for system requirements

The fan is unable to deliver the required volume flow rate at the system's operating pressure. Replace with a fan of adequate capacity to meet system requirements, based on the system curve and duty point. Ensure the new fan operates within its optimal range at the design conditions, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

The fan rotation direction is incorrect, severely reducing performance or potentially causing damage. Check the fan rotation direction against the manufacturer's arrow marking and correct immediately. Verify proper installation and electrical phasing. Test system performance after correction, as per HSG258 Chapter 8.

The fan is producing excessive noise or vibration, indicating operational problems, imbalance, or mechanical wear. Investigate the cause (imbalance, bearing wear, operating outside range, mounting issues). Rectify identified problems. Consider noise attenuation measures if operating correctly but noise remains excessive, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Bends/junctions too close to fan inlet/outlet

Ductwork bends or junctions near the fan create turbulence or swirl, reducing fan efficiency and potentially causing increased static pressure or vibration. Relocate bends to at least five duct diameters downstream of centrifugal fan discharge. Ensure inlet ductwork allows uniform airflow into the fan with minimum turbulence, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

Variable speed drive or control systems are not operating correctly, preventing proper system flow rate adjustment. Service or replace the variable speed controller. Verify control settings and sensor calibrations. Ensure the system can maintain constant static pressure or achieve the required flow rates across the operating range, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Air Cleaner Filter: Filter blockage or excessive pressure drop

Filters have become blocked with collected material, causing excessive pressure drop across the filter and reduced system airflow. Clean or replace blocked filters according to the maintenance schedule. If blockage is recurring rapidly, review the cleaning frequency or filter specification. Check filter cleaning mechanisms are functioning correctly, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

The filter specification does not provide adequate collection efficiency for the particle size distribution or contaminant type being handled. Replace with filters of appropriate efficiency for the application. Consider upgrading to higher efficiency filters or different filter media suitable for the specific contaminant characteristics, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

Filters are absent from the system or are torn, damaged, or incorrectly fitted, allowing contaminant to bypass the cleaning stage. Replace missing or damaged filters immediately with correct specification units. Ensure proper seating and sealing. Verify housing gaskets and seals are intact, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

Automated filter cleaning systems (shaking, reverse pulse, mechanical) are not operating correctly, leading to premature filter blockage. Repair or replace the filter cleaning mechanism. Verify timer settings, compressed air supply (for pulse-jet systems), or mechanical linkages. Test cleaning cycle operation, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

The cyclone separator is not achieving the expected collection efficiency due to wear, damage, or incorrect operation. Inspect for wear on internal surfaces, particularly at the inlet and cone sections. Replace worn components. Verify operating at design flow rates. Check hopper is emptied regularly and discharge valve operates correctly, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

Collected material is not being properly discharged from the air cleaner, leading to re-entrainment or system blockage. Ensure hoppers are emptied regularly. Verify discharge valves, rotary valves, or screws operate correctly. Check collection bins have adequate capacity and are emptied before overfilling, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Air cleaner inappropriate for particle type/size

The air cleaning method or device is not suitable for the particle size distribution, concentration, or properties of the contaminant. Replace with an appropriate air cleaner type: fabric filters for fine dry dusts, cyclones for larger particles or high concentrations, wet scrubbers for flammable or sticky materials, or as appropriate for the specific application, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

Current procedures for changing filters do not prevent the spread of collected hazardous material during maintenance. Develop and implement safe filter change procedures including: appropriate PPE, containment measures, bag-in/bag-out systems where necessary, and waste disposal in accordance with waste regulations. Train maintenance personnel in these procedures, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Instrumentation and Monitoring: Hoods lack simple visual indicators to show operators that adequate airflow is being maintained. Install airflow indicators (typically manometers) on each hood or hood group to display static pressure. Mark the acceptable range clearly. Position indicators where operators can easily see them during normal work, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.

Manometers or other pressure indicators are absent, damaged, or cannot be read clearly. Install or replace manometers to provide clear indication of system performance. Ensure scales are appropriate for the pressure range. Mark the acceptable operating range clearly on the instrument face or adjacent to it, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
No static pressure test points or inadequate marking

The ductwork lacks static pressure tappings at key points, or existing test points are not clearly marked, hindering performance monitoring and fault diagnosis. Install static pressure tappings after each hood, at key duct points, and at components where pressure drop measurement is needed. Mark all test points clearly on the ductwork and on system drawings, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

The system lacks alarms to warn of critical failures such as overpressure in enclosures or filter blockage. Install appropriate alarms: overpressure alarms for enclosures maintained at negative pressure, and filter blockage alarms where filter failure could lead to exposure or system breakdown. Ensure alarms are audible and/or visible to operators, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.

Pressure gauges, flow indicators, or other monitoring instruments are not reading correctly or have not been calibrated. Calibrate or replace instrumentation to ensure accurate readings. Establish a calibration schedule appropriate to the instrument type and criticality. Replace any failed instruments immediately, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Performance indicators not visible to operators

Monitoring instruments are installed in locations where operators cannot see them during normal work, preventing real-time performance checks. Relocate instruments to positions clearly visible from normal working positions, or install remote displays. Ensure instruments are at appropriate heights and well-lit, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.

Systems requiring interlocks (e.g. push-pull systems, spray booths) lack devices to prevent hazardous operation when conditions are unsafe. Install interlocks to: turn off push-pull inlet jets when workpieces obstruct the receiving hood, halt spraying processes in booths during overpressure conditions, or as appropriate for the specific application to prevent unsafe operation, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
Discharge and Makeup Air: The discharge point is positioned where exhausted contaminant could re-enter the building, affect other occupied areas, or cause environmental nuisance. Relocate the discharge point to a safe location away from air inlets, windows, doors, and other occupied areas. Ensure adequate height and distance from sensitive receptors. Consider prevailing wind direction, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

Extracted contaminated air is being discharged directly into occupied workspaces without adequate filtration. Install appropriate air cleaning equipment to reduce contaminant concentration to safe levels before recirculation, or route discharge to external atmosphere away from occupied areas and air inlets, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

Insufficient replacement air is being provided for the volume extracted by the LEV system, causing negative building pressure, reduced system performance, or comfort problems. Provide adequate make-up air supply equal to the volume extracted. Design make-up air system to avoid draughts affecting LEV hood performance. Consider tempering make-up air in cold weather, as per HSG258 Chapter 6 and Chapter 7.

The make-up air system or natural air infiltration is creating draughts that disrupt hood capture zones or cause contaminant leakage from enclosures. Redesign make-up air supply to provide even distribution without draughts affecting work areas. Consider using diffusers, reducing supply velocities, or relocating supply points. Alternatively, increase hood face velocities to resist draughts, as per HSG258 Chapter 6 and Chapter 7.

Air is being returned to the workplace without sufficient filtration or monitoring to ensure contaminant concentrations remain safe. Install high-efficiency filters appropriate for the contaminant. Provide monitoring to verify filter performance (e.g. sensors for filter integrity). Establish a filter maintenance and monitoring schedule. Consider whether recirculation is appropriate for the contaminant type, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

Emissions to atmosphere exceed limits set by environmental permits or regulations. Upgrade air cleaning equipment to achieve required emission standards. Review and improve maintenance of air cleaning systems. Ensure compliance with environmental legislation. Liaise with environmental regulators as necessary, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Documentation: The LEV system lacks a user manual or the existing manual does not provide sufficient information for proper use, checking, and maintenance. Obtain or prepare a comprehensive user manual describing: the LEV system and its operation, checking procedures, maintenance schedules, performance benchmarks, and safety information. Ensure the manual is readily available to users and maintainers, as per HSG258 Chapter 9.

There is no logbook for recording checks, maintenance, and test results, or the logbook is not being kept up to date. Provide a logbook for the system and establish procedures for recording: routine checks, maintenance activities, filter changes, test results, and any defects or repairs. Ensure personnel are trained in logbook use and it is kept with or near the LEV system, as per HSG258 Chapter 9.

No commissioning report exists documenting the system's initial performance, or benchmark data is not available for comparison during thorough examination and test. Obtain the original commissioning report if available, or commission a competent person to establish current performance benchmarks through thorough testing. Document performance standards for all critical parameters, as per HSG258 Chapter 8 and Chapter 10.

Hoods lack labels showing key performance data, or existing labels are damaged or cannot be read. Attach durable labels to each hood displaying: hood identification, required face velocity or static pressure, and any operating instructions. Ensure labels remain legible and replace if damaged, as per HSG258 Chapter 8.

Enclosing rooms or large booths do not display the required clearance time before safe entry after the process stops. Establish the clearance time through testing using smoke tracer or contaminant monitoring. Display the clearance time prominently at the enclosure entrance. Ensure ventilation continues to run for at least the clearance time. Inform all users of the clearance time and its importance, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.

The effective capture zone of capturing hoods is not marked on the workstation, leaving operators unaware of where work must be performed for effective control. Determine the capture zone extent through testing or calculation. Mark the capture zone clearly on the workstation or mark the maximum working distance on the hood. Train operators to work within the marked zone, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.

There are no documented schedules for maintenance activities, leading to inconsistent or inadequate maintenance. Prepare maintenance schedules based on manufacturer recommendations and operational experience, covering: filter changes, lubrication, belt replacement, hopper emptying, duct cleaning, and other routine tasks. Incorporate schedules into the user manual, as per HSG258 Chapter 9.

COSHH risk assessments for the processes controlled by the LEV are absent or do not adequately consider LEV performance requirements. Conduct or update COSHH assessments to identify: substances and their hazards, exposure potential, required control measures, LEV performance standards needed, and monitoring requirements. Ensure assessments inform LEV specifications and maintenance standards, as per HSG258 Appendix 1 and Chapter 5.
Maintenance and Examination: Scheduled maintenance tasks have not been completed, or there is accumulation of minor defects indicating lack of attention. Implement the maintenance schedule without delay. Address all identified defects. Review maintenance procedures and resources to ensure compliance with the schedule. Consider improved supervision or outsourcing if internal resources are insufficient, as per HSG258 Chapter 9 and Chapter 10.

Components show excessive wear that compromises system performance or safety (e.g. fan impellers, duct bends, filter housings). Replace worn parts with components of appropriate specification. If wear is excessive for the service interval, review material selection or consider sacrificial replacement sections for high-wear areas, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

Access to ductwork, filters, or other components requiring regular cleaning or inspection is hazardous or impracticable. Improve access provisions by installing additional access panels, platforms, or safe access equipment. Ensure access complies with work at height regulations and other safety requirements. Consider design modifications if access remains problematic, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

Door seals, duct joint seals, filter housing gaskets, or other sealing components have degraded, causing air leakage. Replace all deteriorated seals, gaskets, and joint materials with appropriate specifications. Check system integrity after replacement. Establish a replacement schedule based on observed deterioration rates, as per HSG258 Chapter 6 and Chapter 7.

Ductwork supports, enclosure framework, or other structural elements show signs of failure, corrosion, or inadequate strength. Assess structural condition and repair or reinforce as necessary. Replace any components that cannot be adequately repaired. Ensure all supports and structures are adequate for the loads and stresses imposed, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

Fan guards, machine guarding, or other safety devices have been removed or are damaged, creating hazards. Replace or repair all guards and safety features immediately. Investigate why guards were removed and address underlying issues. Ensure guards are designed to remain in place and not obstruct necessary access, as per HSG258 Chapter 2 and Chapter 7.

Components have been replaced with parts that do not meet the original specification (e.g. wrong filter type, incorrect fan belt). Replace incorrect parts with components matching the original specification or approved equivalents. Document correct part specifications in the user manual. Ensure maintenance personnel have access to correct parts information, as per HSG258 Chapter 9.

Filter replacement activities are not being documented in the logbook, preventing tracking of filter life and maintenance compliance. Establish procedures requiring logbook entries for all filter changes, recording: date, location, filter type, and person performing the change. Review logbook entries periodically to verify compliance and identify any trends, as per HSG258 Chapter 9.
Operational and Usage: Operators not trained in LEV use

Personnel using the LEV system have not received training on its proper use, limitations, and checking procedures. Provide training to all operators covering: how the LEV system works, correct operating procedures, daily checks to perform, signs of malfunction, and who to report problems to. Document training and refresh periodically, as per HSG258 Chapter 2 and Chapter 5.

The LEV system is not being operated when processes generating contaminant are running. Investigate reasons for non-use (inconvenience, noise, perceived ineffectiveness). Address underlying issues through training, design improvements, or supervision. Establish and enforce procedures requiring LEV operation whenever relevant processes are running, as per HSG258 Chapter 2.

Operators are working beyond the marked capture zone of capturing hoods, or outside the intended working area of partial enclosures. Retrain operators on the importance of working within the defined zone. Verify the capture zone is clearly marked and visible. Consider redesign if the working method cannot be performed within the capture zone, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.

Work processes or materials have changed since the LEV was installed, but the system has not been reviewed to ensure it remains adequate. Conduct a COSHH assessment for the modified process. Review LEV adequacy for new conditions. Commission modifications or replacement LEV as necessary. Establish procedures requiring LEV review before any significant process changes, as per HSG258 Chapter 5.

Some sources of contaminant emission have been missed or are not adequately controlled (e.g. secondary sources such as sack disposal, spillage handling). Identify all emission sources through systematic process observation. Provide LEV or other controls for previously uncontrolled sources. Review work methods to minimise emissions from secondary activities, as per HSG258 Chapter 4.

Workers or materials obstruct hood openings, reducing effective capture area and airflow. Train operators not to obstruct hoods. Review workstation design and modify if necessary to prevent obstruction whilst maintaining access. Consider providing jigs or work positioning aids, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
Multiple processes using shared extraction inappropriately

The LEV system is being used for processes it was not designed to control, or multiple processes operate simultaneously exceeding system capacity. Review system capacity and process requirements. Restrict simultaneous operation to the maximum number of hoods the system was designed for, using interlocks or management controls if necessary. Provide additional extraction if current capacity is genuinely insufficient, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

There is insufficient oversight of LEV operation and maintenance to ensure continued effective use. Assign supervisory responsibility for LEV systems. Establish requirements for regular supervisory checks of LEV operation. Include LEV performance in workplace inspections. Provide supervisors with training on LEV principles and performance indicators, as per HSG258 Chapter 2.
Design Specification: LEV specification inadequate for hazard level

The LEV system design does not provide sufficient control for the hazard level of the substances being handled. Review the exposure benchmark and required control effectiveness (refer to Appendix 2 and Figure 9). Upgrade the LEV system to provide adequate control, which may require increasing degree of enclosure, improving hood design, or adding supplementary controls such as RPE, as per HSG258 Chapter 5.
Control approach does not match COSHH requirements

The control strategy does not align with the COSHH hierarchy of controls or COSHH Essentials guidance for the substance and task. Review the COSHH assessment and required control approach. Consider higher-level controls (elimination, substitution, enclosure) before relying on LEV. Ensure the LEV type and design match the control band requirements, as per HSG258 Chapter 2 and Chapter 5.
No consideration of all emission sources

The LEV specification did not account for all sources of emission in the process, leaving some sources uncontrolled. Identify all emission sources including secondary sources (e.g. spillage, cleaning, material transfer). Specify and install additional LEV or other controls for uncontrolled sources. Review work processes systematically to ensure completeness, as per HSG258 Chapter 4 and Chapter 5.
Process changes needed but not implemented

The LEV designer identified necessary process modifications to enable effective control, but these have not been implemented. Review the design specification and commissioning report to identify required process changes. Implement the necessary modifications through consultation with production and the LEV designer. Do not operate the system until required changes are in place, as per HSG258 Chapter 5.

The LEV installation interferes with normal working methods or creates physical strain, leading to operators not using it correctly or at all. Review the installation with operators and identify specific ergonomic problems. Modify the hood design, position, or workstation layout based on ergonomic principles. Consider prototype testing for complex installations, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
No prototype testing for custom designs

Custom or complex LEV solutions were installed without prototype testing to verify effectiveness with actual work methods. For future installations, conduct prototype testing before final installation. For existing inadequate systems, trial modifications using temporary installations to verify improvements before permanent implementation, as per HSG258 Chapter 6.
Multiple hoods in use exceeding system capacity

More hoods are being used simultaneously than the system was designed to support, causing reduced performance at all extraction points. Determine the maximum number of hoods that can operate simultaneously as per the design. Implement controls (physical isolation, dampers, interlocks, or management procedures) to prevent exceeding this maximum. Consider system capacity upgrade if operational needs genuinely require more simultaneous extractions, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.
Safety and Compliance: The system handles combustible dusts (e.g. wood, organic materials, metals) without adequate measures to prevent ignition or manage explosion risks. Conduct a DSEAR assessment. Implement required measures which may include: bonding and earthing, explosion relief panels, spark detection and suppression, elimination of ignition sources, or system design to contain explosions. Ensure filters and fans are suitable for explosive atmospheres where required, as per HSG258 Chapter 3 and Appendix 1.

The system does not comply with the Dangerous Substances and Explosive Atmospheres Regulations 2002 for operations involving flammable substances or explosive atmospheres. Complete a DSEAR risk assessment. Implement required control measures including: zoning, elimination of ignition sources, equipment suitable for the zone classification, explosion protection measures, and emergency procedures. Ensure documentation is in place, as per HSG258 Appendix 1.

Air cleaners or ductwork handling combustible materials lack adequate explosion relief or suppression systems. Install explosion relief vents, panels, or suppression systems appropriate for the combustible material and system design. Ensure relief is directed to safe areas. Consider flameless venting where external venting is not practicable, as per HSG258 Chapter 3.
Electrical equipment not suitable for atmosphere

Electrical equipment (motors, switches, controls) in potentially explosive atmospheres is not certified for the zone classification. Replace equipment with certified units suitable for the zone (ATEX certified). Review the zone classification if uncertain. Ensure all equipment is properly installed and maintained to preserve certification, as per HSG258 Appendix 1.

Access to LEV components for maintenance, cleaning, or testing involves uncontrolled risks such as work at height, confined space entry, or contact with moving parts. Conduct risk assessments for maintenance activities. Provide safe access equipment (platforms, ladders, harness points). Implement safe systems of work including permits where necessary. Consider design modifications to improve access safety, as per HSG258 Chapter 7 and Appendix 1.

Ductwork penetrations through fire-rated walls or floors have not been properly sealed or protected, reducing fire resistance. Install approved fire dampers, fire-rated seals, or barriers at all fire compartment penetrations. Ensure installations comply with Building Regulations and fire safety requirements. Obtain certification of fire-stopping work where required, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

The LEV system's atmospheric emissions do not comply with environmental permits, planning conditions, or statutory emission limits. Review applicable environmental consents and emission limits. Upgrade air cleaning equipment or modify operations to achieve compliance. Apply for appropriate permits if operating without required authorisations. Liaise with the Environment Agency or local authority as applicable, as per HSG258 Chapter 7.

Safety data sheets are not obtained, risk management measures specified in SDSs are not implemented, or there is inadequate communication up and down the supply chain. Obtain safety data sheets for all substances used. Review and implement risk management measures specified in the SDSs including LEV requirements. Communicate actual use conditions to suppliers. Ensure procedures comply with REACH regulation requirements, as per HSG258 Chapter 5 and Appendix 1.
